.aq_class <- function(...) {
  classes <- unlist(list(...), use.names = FALSE)
  classes <- classes[!is.null(classes) & nzchar(classes)]
  paste(classes, collapse = " ")
}

ui_card <- function(title = NULL, subtitle = NULL, ..., footer = NULL, class = NULL) {
  tags$section(
    class = .aq_class("aq-card", class),
    if (!is.null(title) || !is.null(subtitle)) {
      tags$header(
        class = "aq-card-header",
        if (!is.null(title)) tags$h3(class = "aq-card-title", title),
        if (!is.null(subtitle)) tags$p(class = "aq-card-subtitle", subtitle)
      )
    },
    tags$div(class = "aq-card-body", ...),
    if (!is.null(footer)) tags$footer(class = "aq-card-footer", footer)
  )
}

ui_app_shell <- function(..., theme = "dark") {
  theme <- match.arg(theme, c("light", "dark", "pimp"))

  tagList(
    tags$script(HTML(sprintf(
      "document.body.classList.add('aq-theme-%s');",
      theme
    ))),
    tags$div(class = "aq-app-shell aq-workstation-shell", ...)
  )
}

ui_page <- function(title, subtitle = NULL, ..., actions = NULL, eyebrow = NULL) {
  tags$div(
    class = "aq-page",
    ui_section_header(title, subtitle, actions = actions, eyebrow = eyebrow),
    ...
  )
}

ui_section_header <- function(title, subtitle = NULL, actions = NULL, eyebrow = NULL) {
  tags$header(
    class = "aq-section-header",
    tags$div(
      class = "aq-section-heading",
      if (!is.null(eyebrow)) tags$p(class = "aq-section-eyebrow", eyebrow),
      tags$h2(class = "aq-section-title", title),
      if (!is.null(subtitle)) tags$p(class = "aq-section-subtitle", subtitle)
    ),
    if (!is.null(actions)) tags$div(class = "aq-section-actions", actions)
  )
}

ui_empty_state <- function(title, message = NULL, icon = NULL) {
  tags$div(
    class = "aq-empty-state",
    if (!is.null(icon)) tags$div(class = "aq-empty-state-icon", icon),
    tags$h3(class = "aq-empty-state-title", title),
    if (!is.null(message)) tags$p(class = "aq-empty-state-message", message)
  )
}

ui_loading_state <- function(title = "Working", message = NULL) {
  tags$div(
    class = "aq-loading-state",
    tags$span(class = "aq-loading-indicator", " "),
    tags$div(
      class = "aq-loading-copy",
      tags$strong(title),
      if (!is.null(message)) tags$p(message)
    )
  )
}

  label,
  status = c("success", "warning", "error", "info", "neutral")
) {
  status <- match.arg(status)

  tags$span( ... [truncated]

# AnalyticsShinyApp UX/UI Architecture

## Companion Documents

- [Product Vision](vision/product_vision.md): stable product identity and long-term principles.
- [UI/UX Research Sprint](research/ui_ux_research_sprint.md): living research reference with historical patterns, comparisons, concepts, and exploratory ideas.
- [UX Roadmap](roadmap/ux_roadmap.md): actionable phased implementation roadmap.

## Design Philosophy

AnalyticsShinyApp should feel like a premium analytical workstation: compact, intentional, artifact-aware, and workflow-centered. The app should guide beginners without hiding power from advanced users or developers.

The product goal is not to build a nice Shiny application. The goal is to build professional analytical software that happens to use Shiny as its reactive engine.

Shiny provides:

- reactivity
- state management
- module orchestration
- server communication
- routing

Shiny does not define the UX language. Components, layouts, interaction models, and visual hierarchy are owned by the AnalyticsShinyApp design system and can use custom HTML/CSS/JavaScript when that produces a better analytical experience.

The product principle is:

```text
Simple by default
Powerful when expanded
Inspectable when needed
```

The design inspiration should be closer to professional workspaces such as VS Code, Cursor, Figma, Linear, JetBrains IDEs, Adobe Lightroom, and Power BI Desktop than to a traditional dashboard.

## Visual Language

AnalyticsShinyApp is dark-first. This matches the production AutoPlots and AutoQuant artifact/report defaults, where dark themes are the normal analytical rendering path. Light and pimp modes remain available as explicit alternatives, but the app shell, core tokens, artifact previews, and report-oriented surfaces should treat dark mode as the primary design target.

The shared palette uses:

- deep navy base surfaces for the workstation shell
- elevated blue-navy panels for task surfaces
- blue and cyan accents for focus, active navigation, and analytical action
- green, amber, and red for success, warning, and failure states
- compact 8px radii and dense spacing for operational software rather than marketing composition

Color, spacing, radius, focus, and shadow decisions belong in `www/app.css` tokens. Pages should not define one-off palettes.

## Control Styling

All Shiny controls must visually belong to the workstation shell. Pages may use normal Shin ... [truncated]

### Milestone 2.2: Artifact Inspector

Purpose: provide a persistent selected-object inspector for artifacts.

Dependencies:

- Artifact Gallery
- Artifact Quality Policy
- table preview sidecars
- screenshot metadata
- JSON metadata

Expected UX benefit:

- Users can inspect an artifact without losing context.
- Artifacts feel like durable analytical objects.

Estimated complexity: Medium

Priority: P0

Candidate deliverables:

- screenshot preview
- caption
- source module
- run id
- render target
- completeness score
- diagnostics
- recommendations
- backing table preview
- CSV/JSON sidecar links
- actions: explain, compare, add to story, open source run

### Milestone 2.3: Artifact Filmstrip

Purpose: create Lightroom-style persistent access to recent and important artifacts.

Dependencies:

- Artifact Gallery
- Artifact Inspector
- current project artifact summary

Expected UX benefit:

- Generated evidence is always visible and easy to revisit.
- Users can move through artifacts rapidly.

Estimated complexity: Medium

Priority: P0

Candidate deliverables:

- bottom filmstrip
- recent artifacts
- pinned artifacts
- warning badges
- quality badges
- click-to-inspect behavior

### Milestone 2.4: Artifact Compare

Purpose: compare equivalent artifacts across runs, modules, or model versions.

Dependencies:

- artifact lineage metadata
- run ids
- artifact intent/type metadata
- screenshot/table rendering

Expected UX benefit:

- Users can understand what changed between iterations.

Estimated complexity: High

Priority: P1

Candidate deliverables:

- side-by-side artifact comparison
- metric deltas
- table diffs
- run A vs run B selector

## Phase 3: Mission Control

### Milestone 3.1: Project Health Center

Purpose: surface the state of the entire project in one operational mode.

Dependencies:

- project state summary
- workflow registry
- collector summary
- artifact quality summary
- QA summaries

- Users immediately know what is complete, failing, skipped, or waiting.

Priority: P0

# Task: Mission Control Phase 1 Visual QA and Experience Polish

## Background

Mission Control Phase 1 is now implemented and functionally complete.

Current implementation includes:

- Project Health
- Workflow Status
- Alerts / Open Decisions
- Run Timeline
- Collector Status
- Artifact Quality
- AI Readiness
- QA Integration

Technically it works.

This task is NOT about adding functionality.

This task is about creating an emotional experience.

Artifact Studio should make users want to explore evidence.

Mission Control should make users feel like they are operating a sophisticated analytical system.

When someone opens Mission Control, the desired reaction is NOT:

"Nice dashboard."

The desired reaction is:

"This feels like serious analytical software."

---

# Objective

Perform a complete visual and interaction QA of Mission Control.

Do not begin by changing code.

First evaluate the experience.

Then implement only the highest-value visual improvements.

---

# Evaluate the Following

## Overall Feel

Does this feel like:

Mission Control

or

A collection of status cards?

Be brutally honest.

---

## Project Health

Does Project Health immediately communicate confidence?

Or is it simply showing values?

Questions:

- Does my eye know where to look first?
- Which metrics actually matter?
- Which metrics are just occupying space?
- Does the section create excitement?

---

## Workflow Status

Current workflow cards should answer:

Where am I?

What has completed?

What is running?

What needs attention?

What should I do next?

If they don't, redesign the presentationâ€”not the backend.

---

## Alerts

Alerts should feel operational.

Not error messages.

Review:

priority

ordering

visual hierarchy

actionability

Do warnings naturally attract attention?

Do healthy states fade into the background?

---

## Timeline

The timeline should feel alive.

Not static.

Evaluate:

spacing

density

icons

timestamps

event grouping

chronological readability

Does it tell the story of the project?

---

## Visual Hierarchy

Evaluate:

largest element

second-largest

eye flow

spacing

alignment

contrast

white space

information density

What naturally draws attention?

What gets ignored?

---

## Color Language

Review whether colors communicate:

healthy

warning

critical

running

completed

unknown

Avoid colors that exist only for decoration.

Every color should communicate state.

- ... [truncated]

## 484. 2026-07-08T19:03:04.010Z - current_projectless_megathread - assistant

Completed the Mission Control visual QA and polish pass.

Visual QA findings:
- Strengths: Mission Control had the right data, clear dark workstation styling, strong alert/status primitives, and good operational density.
- Weaknesses: first read still felt like “status cards” more than “command center”; there was no single authoritative mission condition.
- Opportunity: add a top-level mission-state signal that tells the user what matters before they parse the tiles.

Implemented polish:
- Added `ui_mission_state_banner()` in [R/ui_components.R](C:/Users/Bizon/Documents/GitHub/AnalyticsShinyApp/R/ui_components.R:145).
- Added mission status logic and banner placement in [R/page_mission_control.R](C:/Users/Bizon/Documents/GitHub/AnalyticsShinyApp/R/page_mission_control.R:216).
- Added visual treatment for the mission banner, status rails, alert rails, and pulse signal in [www/app.css](C:/Users/Bizon/Documents/GitHub/AnalyticsShinyApp/www/app.css:764).
- Added stylesheet cache-busting in [R/app_ui.R](C:/Users/Bizon/Documents/GitHub/AnalyticsShinyApp/R/app_ui.R) so visual changes actually appear after restart.
- Expanded `qa_mission_control()` to verify visual hierarchy, status presentation, alert semantics, timeline rendering, health consistency, collector presentation, and CSS cache-busting.

Prioritized punch list:
- High: create a first-read mission-state banner. Done.
- High: make status colors communicate health/warning/critical, not decoration. Done via rails and banner state.
- Medium: make workflow cards more operational and less card-wall-like. Partially improved with state rails.
- Medium: make timeline feel more like project history. Existing connected markers retained; future durable run history would deepen this.
- Low: add deeper click actions for alerts/timeline events later. Not implemented per scope.

Validation:
- `qa_mission_control()`: 22 success, 0 failures.
- `qa_analysis_modules_integration()`: 17 success, 1 existing terminology warning, 0 errors.
- `git diff --check`: clean, only LF/CRLF warnings.
- Live browser QA: Mission Control renders mission banner, 8 health tiles, 9 workflow cards, 4 alert cards, 10 timeline entries, no Shiny output errors.

# Task: Build the Global Command Palette (Phase 1)

## Background

Analytics Workstation now has two workstation modes:

- Mission Control
- Artifact Studio

The next foundational capability is a Global Command Palette.

This is NOT just search.

It is the primary action surface for expert users.

The Command Palette should eventually become the fastest way to interact with Analytics Workstation.

Think:

- VS Code
- Cursor
- Linear
- Notion
- Raycast
- Spotlight

This is the workstation's keyboard-first command center.

---

# Philosophy

Everything a user can do with the mouse should eventually be discoverable through the Command Palette.

The Command Palette should become the universal entry point into the application.

The user should eventually be able to think:

"I know what I want to do."

rather than

"I know what page it's on."

---

# Scope

Implement Phase 1 only.

Do NOT implement AI.

Do NOT implement command execution planning.

Do NOT redesign the shell.

Do NOT redesign workstation modes.

Focus on creating a reusable Command Palette framework.

---

# UX

Users should invoke it with:

Ctrl + Shift + P

and optionally

Ctrl + K

if there are no conflicts.

Opening should feel instantaneous.

The background should dim slightly.

Focus should immediately enter the command box.

ESC closes it.

Arrow keys navigate.

ENTER executes.

Mouse remains optional.

---

# Initial Layout

----------------------------------------------------

Command

_________________________________________

Recent

Open Project

Mission Control

Artifact Studio

Workflow

Reports

Export

--------------------------------------------

Suggestions

Open Mission Control

Open Artifact Studio

Generate EDA

Run Model Readiness

Run SHAP

Generate Report

Open Collector

Open Project

Recent Projects

--------------------------------------------

Keyboard Shortcuts

Ctrl+Shift+P

Esc Close

â†‘â†“

Enter

----------------------------------------------------

Simple.

Fast.

Beautiful.

---

# Phase 1 Commands

Initially support navigation only.

Examples:

Mission Control

Artifact Studio

Project

Workflow

Analysis Modules

Export

Code Runner

Reports

Collector

QA

Project Settings

Do not implement analysis execution yet.

---

# Architecture

Create a centralized command registry.

Conceptually:

register_command(

id,

title,

category,

keywords,

icon,

action,

enabled

)

Future modules should register c ... [truncated]

build_app_ui <- function() {
  css_file <- file.path("www", "app.css")
  css_version <- if (file.exists(css_file)) {
    as.integer(file.info(css_file)$mtime)
  } else {
    APP_VERSION
  }
  fluidPage(
    tags$head(tags$link(rel = "stylesheet", type = "text/css", href = paste0("app.css?v=", css_version))),
    ui_app_shell(
      theme = "dark",
      titlePanel("Analytics Workstation"),
      tabsetPanel(
        id = "main_tabs",
        page_mission_control_ui("mission_control"),
        page_project_ui("project"),
        page_data_ui("data"),
        page_plot_builder_ui("plot_builder"),
        page_workflow_ui("workflow"),
        page_analysis_modules_ui("analysis_modules"),
        page_code_runner_ui("code_runner"),
        page_artifact_library_ui("artifact_library"),
        page_layouts_ui("layouts"),
        page_export_ui("export")
      )
    )
  )
}

            "Project loaded, but some plots could not be rebuilt:",

          )
        )
      } else {
        messages <- c(messages, "Project loaded and saved plots rebuilt.")

      messages <- c(
        messages,
        "Project loaded, but source data file was not found. Re-upload the data to rebuild plots."
      )
    }

      layout_type = project_state$layout_type,

      export_name = project_state$export_name
    )

register_command <- function(registry, id, title, category, keywords = character(), icon = ">", action = list(), enabled = TRUE) {
  command <- list(
    id = id,
    title = title,
    category = category,
    keywords = keywords %||% character(),
    icon = icon,
    action = action,
    enabled = isTRUE(enabled)
  )
  registry[[id]] <- command
  registry
}

command_registry <- function() {
  registry <- list()

## 513. 2026-07-08T20:48:56.329Z - current_projectless_megathread - assistant

Implemented the Global Command Palette Phase 1.

What changed:
- Added reusable palette registry/UI/server/QA in [R/command_palette.R](C:/Users/Bizon/Documents/GitHub/AnalyticsShinyApp/R/command_palette.R:1)
- Mounted it in [app.R](C:/Users/Bizon/Documents/GitHub/AnalyticsShinyApp/app.R:54), [R/app_ui.R](C:/Users/Bizon/Documents/GitHub/AnalyticsShinyApp/R/app_ui.R:13), and [R/app_server.R](C:/Users/Bizon/Documents/GitHub/AnalyticsShinyApp/R/app_server.R:945)
- Added aggregate QA registration in [R/module_result.R](C:/Users/Bizon/Documents/GitHub/AnalyticsShinyApp/R/module_result.R)
- Added dark workstation styling in [www/app.css](C:/Users/Bizon/Documents/GitHub/AnalyticsShinyApp/www/app.css)
- Added architecture docs in [docs/command_palette_architecture.md](C:/Users/Bizon/Documents/GitHub/AnalyticsShinyApp/docs/command_palette_architecture.md:1)

Important fix found during browser QA:
- Palette opened and executed commands, but navigation initially did not switch top-level tabs because the module session was namespacing `main_tabs`.
- Fixed by passing the root app session into `command_palette_server(..., navigation_session = session)` and added a QA check for that path.

Validation:
- `qa_command_palette()`: 14 checks, 0 errors
- `qa_analysis_modules_integration()`: 19 modules checked, 0 errors; existing terminology warnings remain
- Browser smoke test passed:
  - `Ctrl+Shift+P` opens palette
  - Search narrows to `Open Artifact Studio`
  - `Enter` navigates to Artifact Studio
  - Recent command appears
  - `Esc` closes palette
  - No browser console warnings/errors
- `git diff --check`: passed; only normal LF/CRLF warnings

Phase 1 stays intentionally navigation-only. No AI actions, planning, workflow graph, Story Builder, Compare, or backend architecture changes were added.


## 514. 2026-07-08T21:04:24.446Z - current_projectless_megathread - user

# Task: Dogfood Analytics Workstation and Eliminate the Top Workflow Friction

## Background

Analytics Workstation now has:

- Mission Control
- Artifact Studio
- Command Palette
- Project Workspace
- Project Artifact Collector
- Render Targets
- Artifact Quality Policy
- Table Artifact Architecture
- Producer Semantics
- Dark-first workstation design
- Comprehensive QA

The architecture is intentionally stable.

This task is NOT about adding another workstation mode.

It is NOT about adding AI.

It is NOT about adding major new features.

The objective is to use the application exactly as a real analyst would.

---

# Objective

Perform an end-to-end dogfooding session.

Start with nothing.

Create or load a project.

Run a realistic analytical workflow.

Generate artifacts.

Inspect evidence.

Generate reports.

Navigate using Mission Control, Artifact Studio, and the Command Palette.

Record every point of friction.

Do not assume current workflows are optimal.

Pretend this is the first time using the software.

---

# Workflow

Perform something similar to:

Create/Open Project

↓

Load Data

↓

EDA

↓

Model Readiness

↓

Model Build

↓

Model Assessment

↓

Model Insights

↓

SHAP

↓

Collector

↓

Reports

↓

Artifact Studio

↓

Mission Control

↓

Export

Navigate naturally throughout the process.

---

# Evaluate

For every step ask:

How many clicks?

How much scrolling?

Was the next action obvious?

Did I know where to go?

Was I ever confused?

Did the software feel fast?

Did it feel enjoyable?

Would a first-time user know what to do?

Would I personally enjoy using this every day?

---

# Produce a Friction Log

For every issue record:

Location

Problem

Severity

Frequency

Suggested Improvement

Estimated Effort

Expected UX Impact

Rank them:

Critical

High

Medium

Low

---

# Fixes

Implement ONLY:

High impact

Low risk

Workflow improvements

Examples:

better defaults

fewer clicks

clearer labels

better empty states

better navigation

better command palette behavior

better inspector defaults

better Mission Control signals

better Artifact Studio flow

Do NOT redesign architecture.

Do NOT implement major new capabilities.

---

# Command Palette

Use the Command Palette whenever practical.

Identify opportunities where users should naturally use it instead of navigation.

Document commands that should exist in Phase 2.

Do not implement them yet unless trivial.

---

# Mission Control

Evaluate whether Mission Control natur ... [truncated]

page_artifact_library_ui <- function(id) {
  ns <- NS(id)

  tabPanel(
    "Artifact Studio",
    ui_page(
      title = "Artifact Studio",
      subtitle = "Explore project evidence through filters, gallery cards, a persistent inspector, and the artifact filmstrip.",
      eyebrow = "Evidence",
      uiOutput(ns("artifact_studio_overview")),
      tags$div(
        class = "aq-artifact-studio",
        tags$aside(
          class = "aq-artifact-studio-left",
          ui_card(
            title = "Filters",
            subtitle = "Narrow the evidence field.",
            selectInput(ns("artifact_collection_filter"), "Collection", choices = c("All"), selected = "All"),
            selectInput(ns("artifact_type_filter"), "Type", choices = c("All"), selected = "All"),
            selectInput(ns("artifact_module_filter"), "Module", choices = c("All"), selected = "All"),
            selectInput(ns("artifact_run_filter"), "Run", choices = c("All"), selected = "All"),
            selectInput(
              ns("artifact_quality_filter"),
              "Quality",
              choices = c("All", "High", "Warning", "Needs Attention"),
              selected = "All"
            ),
            textInput(ns("artifact_search"), "Search", value = "", placeholder = "Search title, module, intent, section")
          ),
          ui_card(
            title = "Project Collections",
            uiOutput(ns("artifact_collection_summary"))
          )
        ),
        tags$main(
          class = "aq-artifact-studio-center",
          ui_card(
            title = "Artifact Gallery",
            subtitle = "Cards are selectable analytical evidence objects.",
            uiOutput(ns("artifact_gallery"))
          )
        ),
        tags$aside(
          class = "aq-artifact-studio-right",
          uiOutput(ns("artifact_inspector"))
        )
      ),
      tags$section(
        class = "aq-artifact-studio-bottom",
        ui_section_header("Filmstrip", "Recently generated artifacts for quick switching.", eyebrow = "Recent Evidence"),
        uiOutput(ns("artifact_filmstrip"))
      )
    )
  )
}

      tryCatch(
        assess_artifact_quality(artifact, render_ta ... [truncated]

      alerts <- mission_state()$alerts
      tags$div(
        class = "aq-alert-queue",
        lapply(alerts, function(alert) {
          ui_alert_card(
            title = alert$title,
            message = alert$message,
            severity = alert$severity,
            source = alert$source
          )
        })
      )
    })

    output$timeline <- renderUI({
      ui_timeline(mission_state()$timeline)
    })

    observeEvent(input$open_artifacts, {
      updateTabsetPanel(session, "main_tabs", selected = "Artifact Studio")
    }, ignoreInit = TRUE)

    observeEvent(input$open_modules, {
      updateTabsetPanel(session, "main_tabs", selected = "Analysis Modules")
    }, ignoreInit = TRUE)

    lapply(workflow_stage_registry(), function(stage) {
      stage_id <- stage$stage_id
      input_id <- paste0("mission_open_", stage_id)
      observeEvent(input[[input_id]], {
        target_page <- stage$page %||% NA_character_
        if (!is.na(target_page) && nzchar(target_page)) {
          updateTabsetPanel(session, "main_tabs", selected = target_page)
        }
      }, ignoreInit = TRUE)
    })
  })
}

  data.table::data.table(
    check = c( ... [truncated]

  tabPanel(
    "Project",

      uiOutput(ns("workspace_progress")),
      ui_workspace_grid(
        columns = "main-sidebar",
        tagList(
          ui_card(
            title = "Workspace Status",
            subtitle = "A compact readout of the current analytical run.",
            uiOutput(ns("project_message_panel")),
            uiOutput(ns("workspace_status")),

              actionButton(ns("load_bundle"), "Load Bundle", class = "btn-secondary btn-sm")
            ),
            level = "advanced"
          )
        )
      )
    )
  )
}

## 543. 2026-07-08T21:12:21.668Z - current_projectless_megathread - assistant

Remaining friction is mostly around warnings/collector manifest wording and report/export discovery, but the highest-impact blockers I can safely fix in this sprint are already addressed. ## 544. 2026-07-08T21:13:41.367Z - current_projectless_megathread - assistant

Dogfooded the workstation end to end with a seeded project: Project load -> evidence restored -> Mission Control -> Artifact Studio -> Layout -> Export, using the Command Palette heavily.

Implemented only low-risk workflow polish.

**Fixed**
- Command Palette ranking now favors direct page/action matches over broad fuzzy matches.
  - `data` now opens Data, not Artifact Studio.
  - `layout` now opens Layout Studio.
  - `export` now opens Export.
- Added `Open Layout Studio` as a clearer command.
- Added root-level `ctx$navigate_to()` so module buttons reliably switch workstation modes.
- Mission Control buttons now route correctly through the root tabset.
- Data Workspace now has direct next-action buttons:
  - `Open Analysis Modules`
  - `Open Workflow`
- Successful module runs now expose direct next actions:
  - `Inspect in Artifact Studio`
  - `Open Export`
  - `Return to Mission Control`
- Project loading now accepts empty Plot Builder collections, which fixes artifact/collector-first projects like the Artifact Studio demo seed.

**Friction Log**
- **High, fixed:** Command Palette fuzzy search could send users to the wrong mode. Expected UX impact: high.
- **High, fixed:** After loading data, the next action was described but not actionable. Expected UX impact: high.
- **High, fixed:** After generating module artifacts, the app did not naturally hand off to Artifact Studio. Expected UX impact: high.
- **High, fixed:** Evidence-only saved projects could fail loading with `plot_configs must be a named list`. Expected UX impact: high.
- **Medium, remaining:** Project load messages include compatibility warnings that visually compete with successful load confirmation.
- **Medium, remaining:** Collector status can show restored artifacts while manifest reads `not_written`, which is technically useful but cognitively noisy.
- **Medium, remaining:** Export still feels more like a separate page than the natural continuation of Artifact Studio/Layout.
- **Low, remaining:** Keyboard shortcut automation can conflict when an input is focused; visible palette launcher works reliably.

**QA**
- `qa_project_load_paths()`: 6 checks, 0 errors
- `qa_command_palette()`: 15 checks, 0 errors
- `qa_mission_control()`: 22 checks, 0 errors
- `qa_analysis_modules_integration()`: 19 modules, 0 errors
- Existing `module_terminology_consistency` warnings remain: 5 warnings, 0 errors
- `git diff --check`: passed, only LF/CRLF warnings

Product vision check: this is getting much cl ... [truncated]


## 545. 2026-07-08T21:27:00.704Z - current_projectless_megathread - user

# Task: Design GenAI Service Contract and Local Provider Architecture

Do not implement Agentic Lab yet.

Design and implement a provider-agnostic GenAI service layer.

Start with local/free providers, but do not hard-code to Ollama.

## Objective

Create a GenAI service contract that allows Analytics Workstation to use local or remote LLM providers through interchangeable adapters.

## Initial Provider Targets

Support or prepare for:

- Ollama
- LM Studio
- llama.cpp server
- OpenAI-compatible local endpoints

Ollama may be the first working adapter.

Other providers may be documented/stubbed if needed.

## Core Principle

The app should call:

genai_chat()
genai_generate()
genai_summarize_artifact()
genai_brief_project()

not provider-specific functions.

Provider adapters handle the details.

## Service Contract

Define:

- provider id
- display name
- base url
- model
- capabilities
- availability check
- list models
- chat
- generate
- structured output if available
- timeout handling
- error handling
- response normalization

## Capabilities

Represent capabilities explicitly:

chat

generate

structured_json

embeddings

vision

streaming

tool_calling

local

remote

free

paid

offline

privacy_preserving

The UI should be able to show what a configured provider can do.

## Configuration

Support app/user configuration for:

provider

base_url

model

temperature

max_tokens

timeout

stream

Do not require paid API keys for local providers.

Do not fail app startup if no provider is configured.

## Local Provider Behavior

If local provider is unavailable:

- app still starts
- GenAI status shows unavailable
- setup guidance is shown
- no hard crash

## Initial Read-Only Use Cases

Implement only low-risk read-only actions:

- summarize selected artifact
- brief current project from metadata
- explain Mission Control alerts
- suggest next analytical action

Do not let GenAI execute app actions.

## Context Policy

Do not dump full datasets or huge tables by default.

Use the hierarchy:

- project metadata
- collector manifest summary
- artifact captions
- quality metadata
- diagnostics
- recommendations
- preview tables

## 557. 2026-07-08T23:14:36.691Z - current_projectless_megathread - user

# Task: Run Ollama GenAI Smoke Test and Information Transfer Experiment

## Objective

Verify whether the local GenAI service hook is operational with Ollama, then run a small information-transfer experiment using the seeded Artifact Studio project.

Do not implement new architecture unless a small bug blocks the test.

---

## Step 1: Check Ollama Availability

From R:

source("app.R")

Run:

qa_genai_service_contract()

Then check:

genai_available(provider = "ollama")
genai_list_models(provider = "ollama")

If Ollama is not running, report that clearly.

Do not crash the app.

If no models are available, report setup guidance such as:

ollama pull llama3.1
ollama pull mistral
ollama pull qwen2.5

Use whatever local model is already available if any exist.

---

## Step 2: Basic Chat Smoke Test

Run a minimal prompt through Ollama using the provider abstraction.

Use:

genai_chat_with_telemetry()

or the current equivalent wrapper.

Prompt:

"Summarize this in one sentence: Analytics Workstation treats artifacts as evidence and the collector as project memory."

Verify:

- response returned
- telemetry attached
- provider recorded
- model recorded
- latency recorded
- token estimates recorded
- no hard failure if provider is unavailable

---

## Step 3: Load Seeded Artifact Studio Project

Load the seeded demo project if available:

exports/artifact_studio_demo/artifact_studio_demo_project.rds

If it does not exist, run:

create_artifact_studio_demo_project()

Verify:

- project loads
- artifacts exist
- collector manifest exists
- plot artifacts have screenshot paths
- table artifacts have table sidecars

---

## Step 4: Run Small Information Transfer Experiment

Pick one plot artifact and one table artifact.

For the plot artifact, test these context strategies:

- caption_metadata
- screenshot_only
- screenshot_caption
- screenshot_caption_preview
- structured_json_summary, if available

For the table artifact, test:

- caption_metadata
- table_preview_only
- full_table, only if small enough
- structured_json_summary, if available
- balanced

Use the same question per artifact.

Plot question:

"What are the main analytical takeaways from this artifact? Include any limitations."

Table question:

"What are the main analytical takeaways from this table? Include any limitations."

Run each through:

genai_summarize_artifact()

or the closest existing read-only helper.

---

## Step 5: Store Experiment Results

Create an experiment output table with:

- ... [truncated]

- Provider abstraction worked through `genai_chat_with_telemetry()`

**Basic Chat**
Prompt succeeded.

Response:
> The Analytics Workstation conceptually regards artifacts as pieces of evidence and the Collector as a repository for storing and recalling project-related information.

Telemetry attached:
- provider: `ollama`
- model: `mistral:latest`
- estimated input tokens: `30`
- reported input tokens: `29`
- latency: `8000 ms`

**Seeded Project**
The existing seeded project was present, but stale for this experiment:
- It loaded.
- Plot screenshots existed.
- Table sidecar/manifest verification failed from the saved state.

I refreshed it with `create_artifact_studio_demo_project()`:
- artifacts: `36`
- screenshots: `52`
- table sidecar files: `178`
- collector manifest: present

**Experiment Outputs**
- [ollama_smoke_test_results.csv](C:/Users/Bizon/Documents/GitHub/AnalyticsShinyApp/exports/genai_experiments/ollama_smoke_test_results.csv)
- [ollama_smoke_test_responses.json](C:/Users/Bizon/Documents/GitHub/AnalyticsShinyApp/exports/genai_experiments/ollama_smoke_test_responses.json)

**Experiment Summary**
Selected artifacts:
- Plot: `Target Distribution`
- Table: `Data Description`

All 10 strategy calls succeeded.

Token/latency highlights:
- Plot strategies were cheap: ~141-260 estimated input tokens, ~2.4-3.4s latency.
- Table strategies were more expensive:
  - `caption_metadata`: 156 estimated tokens
  - `table_preview_only`: 572
  - `balanced`: 821
  - `full_table`: 1862
- `full_table` worked but was much more expensive, as expected.
- For the plot, `screenshot_caption` / `screenshot_caption_preview` looked most useful.
- For the table, `table_preview_only` and `balanced` were more useful than caption-only; `full_table` added cost without obvious proportional gain in this small test.

**Small Bug Fixed**
Added provider-agnostic wrappers:
- `genai_available()`
- `genai_list_models()`

These call the existing provider abstraction and are not Ollama-specific.

**QA**
- `qa_genai_service_contract()`: 18 checks, 0 errors
- `qa_analysis_modules_integration()`: 20 modules, 0 errors
- Existing terminology warnings remain isolated
- `git diff --check`: passed


## 560. 2026-07-09T00:17:25.491Z - current_projectless_megathread - user

# Task: Build Robust GenAI Information Transfer Experiment Harness

## Objective

Expand the Ollama smoke test into a reusable experiment harness for comparing artifact context strategies.

The goal is to learn which representation communicates the most useful information to an LLM for the lowest cost.

Do not implement Agentic Lab.

Do not implement autonomous actions.

This is controlled experimentation and telemetry.

---

## Experiment Dimensions

Support experiments across:

- artifact type
- artifact id
- question type
- context strategy
- provider
- model

Examples:

artifact_type:
- plot
- table
- narrative
- diagnostic
- recommendation
- json

context_strategy:
- caption_metadata
- screenshot_only
- screenshot_caption
- screenshot_caption_preview
- table_preview_only
- full_table
- structured_json_summary
- balanced

question_type:
- summarize
- limitations
- key_findings
- explain_for_executive
- explain_for_data_scientist
- identify_risks
- suggest_next_action

---

## Core Functions

Implement reusable helpers such as:

run_genai_artifact_experiment()

run_genai_project_experiment()

build_genai_experiment_grid()

score_genai_experiment_result()

write_genai_experiment_results()

qa_genai_experiment_harness()

Use existing GenAI provider abstractions.

Do not hard-code Ollama except as the default local test provider.

---

## Inputs

Experiment runner should accept:

- project object or project path
- artifact ids, or "sample"
- artifact types
- context strategies
- question types
- provider
- model
- max artifacts per type
- max full table rows
- output directory
- dry_run

---

## Sampling

Support sampling artifacts from a project.

Examples:

- first N plots
- first N tables
- highest quality artifacts
- warning artifacts
- critical importance artifacts

Keep defaults small and safe.

---

## Context Safety

Do not send full datasets by default.

For full_table strategy:

- only allow when table is below a safe row/column threshold
- otherwise downgrade to table_preview_only or balanced
- record the downgrade in telemetry

---

## Telemetry

For every call record:

- experiment_id
- run_id
- timestamp
- artifact_id
- artifact_type
- artifact_title
- question_type
- provider
- model
- context_strategy_requested
- context_strategy_used
- included_components
- estimated_input_tokens
- reported_input_tokens
- estimated_output_tokens
- reported_output_tokens
- total_estimated_tokens
- latency_ms
- success
- error
- response_excerpt
- full_respo ... [truncated]


## 561. 2026-07-09T00:17:43.475Z - current_projectless_megathread - tool_output

Tool output:
Exit code: 0
Wall time: 0.5 seconds
Output:

The adapter is prepared for `/health` and `/completion`.

### OpenAI-Compatible Endpoint

The `openai_compatible` provider exists for local or remote APIs that follow the OpenAI chat-completions shape. Remote providers may require keys later, but Phase 1 does not require a paid provider.

## Read-Only Use Cases

Implemented service functions:

- `genai_summarize_artifact()`
- `genai_brief_project()`
- `genai_explain_alerts()`
- `genai_suggest_next_action()`

These functions generate text only. They do not execute commands or change project state.

## Information Transfer Efficiency

Analytics Workstation instruments GenAI calls so we can learn which artifact representations communicate the most useful information to an LLM for the lowest cost.

Every instrumented GenAI call records:

- `context_strategy`
- `included_components`
- `estimated_input_tokens`
- `reported_input_tokens` when the provider reports usage
- `latency_ms`
- `provider`
- `model`
- `output_quality_score` placeholder
- `accuracy_score` placeholder
- `user_rating` placeholder

Tracked context components:

- `screenshot`
- `caption`
- `metadata`
- `diagnostics`
- `recommendations`
- `table_preview`
- `full_table`
- `json_summary`
- `sidecar_reference`

Initial comparison strategies:

- `screenshot_only`
- `caption_metadata`
- `screenshot_caption`
- `table_preview_only`
- `full_table`
- `screenshot_caption_preview`
- `structured_json_summary`
- `balanced`

The purpose is empirical. We should not assume screenshots are always better, and we should not assume structured data is always better. Different artifact types may have different tradeoff frontiers.

Future UI can use this telemetry to recommend context strategies based on constraints:

- minimize tokens
- maximize accuracy
- balanced
- local/private
- fastest response

Automatic strategy optimization is intentionally not implemented yet.

## UI Surfaces

Provider status and read-only actions appear in:

- Mission Control
- Artifact Studio Inspector
- Project Workspace

The UI shows provider, model, availability, capabilities, and local/privacy status.

## Future Agentic Lab Integration

Future Agentic Lab work should use this service layer rather than introducing provider-specific calls. Agentic behavior must remain separate from this contract and should add explicit permission, planning, preview-before-commit, and action policies before any executable actions are allowed.
. : Fi ... [truncated]

genai_brief_project <- function(ctx, config = genai_config(), context_strategy = "balanced") {
  context <- genai_build_project_context(ctx, strategy = context_strategy)
  included_components <- attr(context, "included_components") %||% genai_context_strategy(context_strategy)$included_components
  prompt <- paste(
    "Brief the current Analytics Workstation project from metadata, collector summary, artifact captions, diagnostics, and recommendations.",
    "Do not request or infer full raw data. Keep it concise and decision-oriented.",
    genai_context_json(context),
    sep = "\n\n"
  )
  genai_generate_with_telemetry(
    prompt,
    config = config,
    context_strategy = context_strategy,
    included_components = included_components,
    call_type = "brief_project"
  )
}

genai_explain_alerts <- function(alerts, config = genai_config(), context_strategy = "structured_json_summary") {
  included_components <- genai_context_strategy(context_strategy)$included_components
  prompt <- paste(
    "Explain these Mission Control alerts for an analyst. Clarify why each matters and what to inspect next. Do not execute actions.",
    genai_context_json(alerts),
    sep = "\n\n"
  )
  genai_generate_with_telemetry(
    prompt,
    config = config,
    context_strategy = context_strategy,
    included_components = included_components,
    call_type = "explain_alerts"
  )
}

## 565. 2026-07-09T00:26:52.746Z - current_projectless_megathread - tool_output

Tool output:
Exit code: 0
Wall time: 0.4 seconds
Output:

- `genai_summarize_artifact()`
- `genai_brief_project()`
- `genai_explain_alerts()`
- `genai_suggest_next_action()`

These functions generate text only. They do not execute commands or change project state.

## Information Transfer Efficiency

Analytics Workstation instruments GenAI calls so we can learn which artifact representations communicate the most useful information to an LLM for the lowest cost.

Every instrumented GenAI call records:

- `context_strategy`
- `included_components`
- `estimated_input_tokens`
- `reported_input_tokens` when the provider reports usage
- `latency_ms`
- `provider`
- `model`
- `output_quality_score` placeholder
- `accuracy_score` placeholder
- `user_rating` placeholder

Tracked context components:

- `screenshot`
- `caption`
- `metadata`
- `diagnostics`
- `recommendations`
- `table_preview`
- `full_table`
- `json_summary`
- `sidecar_reference`

Initial comparison strategies:

- `screenshot_only`
- `caption_metadata`
- `screenshot_caption`
- `table_preview_only`
- `full_table`
- `screenshot_caption_preview`
- `structured_json_summary`
- `balanced`

The purpose is empirical. We should not assume screenshots are always better, and we should not assume structured data is always better. Different artifact types may have different tradeoff frontiers.

Future UI can use this telemetry to recommend context strategies based on constraints:

- minimize tokens
- maximize accuracy
- balanced
- local/private
- fastest response

Automatic strategy optimization is intentionally not implemented yet.

## UI Surfaces

Provider status and read-only actions appear in:

- Mission Control
- Artifact Studio Inspector
- Project Workspace

The UI shows provider, model, availability, capabilities, and local/privacy status.

## Future Agentic Lab Integration

Future Agentic Lab work should use this service layer rather than introducing provider-specific calls. Agentic behavior must remain separate from this contract and should add explicit permission, planning, preview-before-commit, and action policies before any executable actions are allowed.

genai_experiment_prompt <- function(artifact, question_type, context_strategy) {
  context <- genai_build_artifact_context(artifact, strategy = context_strategy)
  included_components <- attr(context, "included_components") %||% genai_context_strategy(context_strategy)$included_components
  prompt <- paste(
    "You are reviewing one Analytics Workstation artifact.",
    "Use only the provided artifact context. Do not invent values not present in the context.",
    genai_question_prompt(question_type),
    genai_context_json(context),
    sep = "\n\n"
  )
  list(prompt = prompt, context = context, included_components = included_components)
}

score_genai_experiment_result <- function(result) {
  text <- result$value$text %||% ""
  list(
    estimated_output_tokens = genai_estimate_tokens(text),
    response_excerpt = substr(gsub("[\r\n]+", " ", text), 1L, 600L),
    output_quality_score = NA_real_,
    accuracy_score = NA_real_,
    user_rating = NA_real_,
    reviewer_notes = NA_character_
  )
}

) {

# Task: Build Plot-Type-Aware GenAI Context Strategy Research Framework

## Background

We now have:

- GenAI provider abstraction
- Ollama local provider
- local vision model support
- true image payload telemetry
- image_reference_only detection
- context strategy experiment harness
- token/latency telemetry
- CSV/JSON/summary outputs

The first true local image-vs-data study proved the machinery works, but the next level is more important:

We need to learn which context strategy works best by artifact type, not globally.

A plot screenshot may be extremely efficient for one plot type and weak for another.

A box plot may compress distributional information well visually, but exact quantiles may require structured data.

A heatmap may communicate pattern visually, but exact values may require a table.

A SHAP dependence plot may communicate nonlinear shape better visually than through table previews.

The goal is to move toward deterministic and probabilistic rules for context strategy selection.

---

# Objective

Create a plot-type-aware and provenance-aware GenAI context strategy research framework.

This should allow Analytics Workstation to learn:

- which context strategy is most efficient by artifact type
- which strategy is most accurate by artifact type
- which strategy is fastest
- when images are worth the cost
- when tables/JSON are better
- when hybrid strategies are best
- how confident we are in those recommendations

Do not implement automatic product optimization yet.

This is research infrastructure.

---

# Core Concepts

## Artifact Family

Add or infer artifact_family.

Examples:

- bar
- horizontal_bar
- variable_importance
- boxplot
- histogram
- heatmap
- correlation_matrix
- scatter
- shap_dependence
- shap_importance
- shap_interaction
- line
- area
- trend
- faceted_plot
- table_ranking
- table_metrics
- table_diagnostics
- table_correlation
- narrative
- recommendation
- diagnostic

Use explicit producer metadata when available.

Fallback to inference from artifact title/type/module/caption.

Record policy_source:

- explicit
- inferred
- unknown

---

## Context Provenance

Record where each context component came from.

Fields:

- caption_source
- metadata_source
- diagnostics_source
- recommendations_source
- narrative_source
- table_preview_source
- json_summary_source
- screenshot_source

Allowed values:

- deterministic
- model_computed
- ai_generated
- user_suppl ... [truncated]

build_genai_experiment_grid <- function(
  project,
  artifact_ids = "sample",
  artifact_types = c("plot", "table"),
  context_strategies = c("caption_metadata", "balanced"),
  question_types = "summarize",
  provider = "ollama",
  model = NULL,
  vision_enabled = FALSE,
  max_image_bytes = 2500000L,
  max_image_count = 1L,
  max_artifacts_per_type = 1L,
  sampling = "first",
  project_path = NULL
) {
  loaded_project <- genai_load_experiment_project(project)
  selected <- genai_sample_experiment_artifacts(
    genai_project_artifacts(loaded_project),
    artifact_ids = artifact_ids,
    artifact_types = artifact_types,
    max_artifacts_per_type = max_artifacts_per_type,
    sampling = sampling
  )
  rows <- list()
  for (artifact in selected) {
    for (strategy in context_strategies) {
      for (question_type in question_types) {
        rows[[length(rows) + 1L]] <- data.table::data.table(
          artifact_id = artifact$artifact_id %||% "",
          artifact_type = artifact$artifact_type %||% artifact$type %||% "",
          artifact_title = artifact$label %||% artifact$title %||% artifact$artifact_id %||% "",
          question_type = question_type,
          provider = provider,
          model = model %||% NA_character_,
          context_strategy_requested = strategy
        )
      }
    }
  }
  grid <- data.table::rbindlist(rows, fill = TRUE)
  attr(grid, "project") <- loaded_project
  attr(grid, "project_path") <- project_path
  grid
}

genai_experiment_prompt <- function(artifact, question_type, context_strategy) {
  context <- genai_build_artifact_context(artifact, strategy = context_strategy)
  included_components <- attr(context, "included_components") %||% genai_context_strategy(context_strategy)$included_components
  prompt <- paste(
    "You are reviewing one Analytics Workstation artifact.",
    "Use only the provided artifact context. Do not invent values not present in the context.",
    genai_question_prompt(question_type),
    genai_context_json(context),
    sep = "\n\n"
  )
  list(prompt = prompt, context = context, included_components = included_components)
}

score_genai_experiment_result <- function(result) {
  text <- result$value$text %||% ""
  list(
    estimated_output_tokens = genai_estimate_tokens(text),
    response_excerpt = substr(gsub("[\r\n]+", " ", text), 1L, 600L), ... [truncated]

## 571. 2026-07-09T02:49:20.363Z - current_projectless_megathread - user

# Task: Run Targeted Plot-Type-Aware GenAI Context Strategy Study

## Objective

Run a targeted real GenAI context strategy study using the new plot-type-aware research framework.

This is a controlled research run, not a product feature.

Do not implement new architecture unless a small bug blocks the study.

---

## Setup

Use the seeded Artifact Studio project.

If stale or missing, regenerate it with:

create_artifact_studio_demo_project()

Use local Ollama.

Preferred model:

llava:latest

If llama3.2-vision is available and the installed Ollama version supports it, it may be used instead.

Record provider/model/capabilities.

---

## Study Scope

Keep the study targeted.

Use:

- one local vision-capable model
- 3 to 5 artifact families
- approximately 3 context strategies per family
- 2 question types
- repeat_count = 2

Target artifact families if available:

- shap_dependence
- boxplot
- heatmap or correlation_matrix
- variable_importance or shap_importance
- table_metrics or table_ranking

If a target family is unavailable in the seeded project, record it as a coverage gap.

Do not fabricate artifacts.

---

## Context Strategies

For plot families, test:

- caption_metadata
- screenshot_caption
- screenshot_caption_preview
- structured_json_summary if available

For table families, test:

- caption_metadata
- table_preview_only
- balanced
- full_table only when safe

Vision strategies must record:

- image_payload_used
- image_reference_only
- vision_downgrade_reason

If image payloads are not truly used, flag the result clearly.

---

## Question Types

Use exactly two question types for this targeted run:

1. key_findings

Prompt:
"What are the key analytical findings from this artifact?"

2. limitations

Prompt:
"What limitations or caveats should be considered when interpreting this artifact?"

Keep question wording consistent across strategies.

---

## Outputs

Write results under:

exports/genai_experiments/context_strategy_targeted_study_<timestamp>/

Include:

- results.csv
- responses.json
- summary.md
- family_comparison.md
- strategy_recommendations.csv
- open_questions.md

---

## Report

Summarize:

- artifact families tested
- coverage gaps
- provider/model used
- number of calls
- success/failure count
- true image payload count
- average latency by strategy
- average token estimate by strategy
- observed differences by artifact family
- whether preliminary deterministic rules look directionally right
- recommended next study

Do ... [truncated]

Setup:

- Model: `llava:latest`
- Vision capable: yes
- Seeded Artifact Studio project used
- Question types: `key_findings`, `limitations`
- `repeat_count = 2`

- `correlation_matrix`: unavailable in seed

- `table_ranking`: available via SHAP ranking table, but not separately selected to keep scope targeted

Run results:
- Calls: 80
- Successes: 80
- Failures: 0
- True image payload calls: 24
- Image reference-only calls: 0

Observed telemetry:
- Plot screenshot strategies used true image payloads.
- Table strategies stayed text/table based.
- `caption_metadata` was usually cheapest by estimated tokens.
- Screenshot strategies had higher latency, consistent with local vision processing.
- `full_table` remained guarded and was only used where safe.

Directional rule check:
- Vision strategies require `image_payload_used = TRUE`: confirmed.
- Text/table artifacts should not claim image payloads: confirmed.
- Full tables should remain guarded: confirmed.
- Whether screenshot strategies are better than metadata/JSON requires manual scoring.

QA:

- `qa_genai_vision_support()`: 8 success

# Task: Build Evidence Routing Policy and Learning Observability Layer

## Background

Analytics Workstation now has:

- Artifact Model
- Project Artifact Collector
- Render Targets
- Artifact Quality Policy
- Table Artifact Architecture
- Producer Semantics
- GenAI Provider Abstraction
- Local Vision Support
- GenAI Context Strategy Experiments
- Artifact-Family Context Strategy Research

The next step is to make GenAI context selection more professional.

The app should not blindly send all artifacts to an LLM.

It should build an evidence plan.

The evidence plan should decide:

- what to include
- what to exclude
- what to summarize
- what to deep dive
- what to keep as sidecar reference
- why each decision was made

This should begin conservatively, with transparent rules and observable outcomes.

Over time, the system should collect enough evidence to refine routing decisions.

---

# Objective

Implement a conservative Evidence Routing Policy.

This policy should estimate the expected utility of artifacts for a given user question and context budget.

It should support future learning, but should not pretend to be fully intelligent yet.

The first implementation should be:

- rule-based
- conservative
- explainable
- configurable
- telemetry-rich
- learning-ready

---

# Core Concept

Before any LLM call, build an Evidence Plan.

Conceptually:

Question

â†“

Evidence Routing Policy

â†“

Evidence Plan

â†“

Context Builder

â†“

LLM

â†“

Answer

â†“

Telemetry / Feedback

â†“

Future Policy Refinement

---

# Evidence Plan

Create a standard Evidence Plan object.

Each plan should include:

- question
- task type
- user constraints
- token budget
- latency budget
- accuracy preference
- privacy preference
- selected artifacts
- excluded artifacts
- sidecar-only artifacts
- deep-dive artifacts
- context strategy per artifact
- reasoning for each inclusion/exclusion
- expected utility
- expected token cost
- confidence
- fallback strategy

---

# Utility Model

Create a conservative first-pass utility score.

Suggested components:

artifact_utility =
task_relevance
Ã— trustworthiness
Ã— novelty
Ã— expected_insight_gain
Ã— user_preference_weight
Ã· estimated_context_cost

Where possible, compute or approximate:

## Task Relevance

How relevant is this artifact to the question?

Signals:

- artifact family
- analytical intent
- module
- title/caption keywords
- user question keywords
- artifact ... [truncated]


## 574. 2026-07-09T05:13:38.253Z - current_projectless_megathread - tool_output

Output:
# Task: Create the Context Optimization Policy

## Background

Analytics Workstation has evolved beyond simple prompt engineering.

The system now contains:

- Artifact Model
- Project Artifact Collector
- Render Targets
- Artifact Quality Policy
- Table Artifact Architecture
- Producer Semantics
- Evidence Routing Policy
- GenAI Context Strategy Research

The next architectural layer is Context Optimization.

This becomes one of the core architectural contracts of Analytics Workstation.

It should be treated as a first-class library artifact.

It should guide every future interaction with GenAI.

---

# Philosophy

Context is not free.

Neither are:

tokens

latency

attention

reasoning

privacy

Every piece of information sent to an LLM has a cost.

The objective is not to minimize tokens.

The objective is to maximize analytical information transfer while minimizing unnecessary cost.

---

# Core Principle

Never spend probabilistic intelligence on deterministic knowledge.

Deterministic reasoning should always execute first.

Only uncertainty should consume probabilistic reasoning.

---

# Context Optimization Hierarchy

Layer 1

Deterministic Knowledge

Examples:

- constant variables
- near-zero variance
- missingness
- sparse groups
- correlation
- artifact quality
- screenshot availability
- render target
- producer metadata
- collector metadata
- routing profile
- provider capabilities
- context size estimation
- token estimation
- image capability
- safety limits

These should never require GenAI.

---

Layer 2

Evidence Routing

Using deterministic information:

Estimate:

task relevance

trustworthiness

novelty

expected insight gain

context cost

Assign routing levels.

Build Evidence Plans.

Still deterministic.

---

Layer 3

Probabilistic Routing

Only if deterministic routing cannot confidently choose.

Examples:

- redundant artifact detection
- semantic overlap
- evidence prioritization
- artifact usefulness estimation
- routing uncertainty

This may use:

local GenAI

or

paid GenAI

depending on configuration.

The goal is to reduce the evidence search space.

Not answer the user's question.

---

Layer 4

Probabilistic Reasoning

Only after evidence has been selected.

The chosen model reasons over the optimized evidence bundle.

This is the expensive step.

Everything before this should reduce its workload.

---

Layer 5

Learning

Observe outcomes.

Do not automatically mut ... [truncated]

Output:
# Analytics Workstation Product Vision

## Product Identity

Analytics Workstation is an evidence-centered analytical operating environment.

It is not primarily a dashboard.

It is not primarily a Shiny app.

Shiny is the reactive engine: state, orchestration, server communication, and routing. The product identity is larger than the implementation substrate. Analytics Workstation exists to help users create, inspect, preserve, explain, and communicate analytical understanding across an entire project lifecycle.

## Core Thesis

The project is the world.

Modules are producers.

Artifacts are evidence.

The Project Artifact Collector is memory.

AI reasons over evidence.

Human reports and LLM artifacts are different render targets.

The workstation exists to create understanding rather than dashboards.

## Operating Model

Analytics work should feel like moving through one durable project environment:

```text
Project
-> Data
-> Workflow
-> Analysis
-> Artifacts
-> Collector
-> Reports
-> AI
```

Users should not feel like they are jumping between disconnected pages. They should feel like they are switching operational modes inside one analytical environment.

## Workstation Modes

Mission Control, Artifact Studio, and Agentic Lab are Workstation Modes, not standalone pages.

- Mission Control surfaces project health, workflow state, run status, warnings, collector status, QA, and AI readiness.
- Artifact Studio treats artifacts as tangible analytical evidence: searchable, inspectable, comparable, composable, and reportable.
- Agentic Lab allows AI to plan, inspect, explain, and execute over project evidence with preview-before-commit controls.

This mode model is closer to Lightroom modules, IDE workspaces, or professional trading terminals than a traditional web dashboard.

## Architectural Alignment

The product vision reinforces the implemented architecture:

- Artifact Model: all analytical outputs should become standardized artifacts wherever practical.
- Render Targets: human report rendering and LLM DOCX rendering are separate target decisions, not separate analytical truths.
- Project Artifact Collector: project-level evidence aggregation belongs to the project, not individual modules.
- Artifact Quality Policy: every artifact should be evaluated consistently for completeness, metadata, captions, screenshots, tables, diagnostics, recommendations, and JSON where available.
- Table Artifact ... [truncated]

# Task: Formalize Evidence Strategy UX and Technical Configuration

## Background

Analytics Workstation now has:

- Context Optimization Policy
- Evidence Routing Policy
- GenAI context strategy research
- local/free GenAI provider support
- vision-model image-vs-data experiments
- observability hooks
- routing profiles

The next step is to make this usable for both:

1. MBA / business users who need simple decision-oriented controls.
2. Technical users who want precise configuration.

The system should support full evidence explosion when the decision is critical or cost is irrelevant, but should also make efficient evidence selection easy when token cost, latency, or privacy matter.

---

# Core Philosophy

This is the same logic as MMM or optimization:

For any decision, quantify:

- marginal benefit
- marginal cost
- contextual utility
- constraints
- uncertainty

The software should not blindly send everything.

It should help users choose an evidence strategy that fits the decision context.

---

# Objective

Create a user-facing Evidence Strategy layer that maps simple business-friendly choices to technical routing configuration.

This should become the UX bridge between:

Business intent

â†“

Context Optimization Policy

â†“

Evidence Routing Policy

â†“

GenAI context construction

---

# Business-Friendly Evidence Strategies

Define high-level strategies such as:

## Efficient

Fastest and lowest cost.

Use only the highest-value evidence.

Best for:

- quick reads
- exploratory questions
- low-stakes decisions
- local/private usage

## Balanced

Default mode.

Enough evidence for sound judgment without excessive cost.

Best for:

- normal business decisions
- routine model interpretation
- project briefings

## Thorough

Broader evidence inclusion.

More diagnostics, caveats, and supporting views.

Best for:

- stakeholder-facing recommendations
- deeper analytical review
- uncertain findings

## Critical Decision

Evidence explosion allowed.

Redundancy allowed.

More diagnostics, more screenshots, more tables, more caveats.

Best for:

- high-stakes business decisions
- production model approval
- executive signoff
- expensive media or pricing decisions

## Cost Is Irrelevant

Use everything reasonable.

Best for:

- offline/local runs
- nearly free token environments
- final review
- research/deep audit

---

# Technical Configuration Mapping

Each business strategy should map to tech ... [truncated]

## 581. 2026-07-09T05:50:31.618Z - current_projectless_megathread - user

# Task: Create the Information Encoding Policy

## Background

Analytics Workstation already distinguishes render targets:

- Human Reports
- LLM DOCX
- Artifact Studio
- Collector

A new architectural realization has emerged.

Render target and information encoding are not the same concept.

The same analytical artifact should be encoded differently depending on its consumer.

This becomes another core architectural policy.

---

# Philosophy

The purpose of an analytical artifact is not beauty.

The purpose is efficient transfer of analytical information.

Different consumers have different optimal encodings.

Human

↓

Interactive understanding

LLM

↓

Information density

Thumbnail

↓

Recognition

Executive

↓

Decision support

The encoding should optimize for the consumer.

---

# Core Principle

Separate:

Analytical Artifact

↓

Information Encoding

↓

Render Target

The analytical artifact remains identical.

Only its encoding changes.

---

# Consumer Types

Initially support:

Human

LLM

Thumbnail

Presentation

Executive

Developer

Future consumers may be added.

---

# Human Encoding

Optimize for:

readability

visual hierarchy

spacing

larger fonts

interaction

presentation quality

progressive disclosure

exploration

---

# LLM Encoding

Optimize for:

information density

annotation density

compact legends

smaller fonts when still readable

more labels

more reference lines

combined analytical views

higher data-to-pixel ratio

less decorative whitespace

maximum information transfer

Visual beauty is secondary.

Analytical density is primary.

---

# Thumbnail Encoding

Optimize for:

recognition

visual identity

fast scanning

artifact browsing

Do not attempt to communicate every analytical detail.

---

# Executive Encoding

Optimize for:

decision support

major findings

risk

recommendations

minimal statistical detail

---

# Developer Encoding

Optimize for:

debugging

traceability

metadata

diagnostics

raw analytical detail

---

# Composite Analytical Views

Document a new concept.

Composite plots exist to increase information transfer.

Examples:

Bar + Line

Importance + Cumulative

Histogram + Density

Scatter + Smoother

Scatter + Marginals

SHAP Dependence + Binned Mean

Boxplot + Mean

Trend + Confidence Bands

Trend + Anomalies

These are not decorative.

They intentionally increase analytical density.

---

# AutoPlots Evolution

Document AutoPlots V2 direction.

Do NOT implement.

Future plot APIs should ... [truncated]

  # Transformation
  if(YVarTrans != "Identity") {
    dt1 <- AutoTransformationCreate(data = dt1, ColumnNames = YVar, Methods = YVarTrans)$Data
  }

  # Transformation
  if(XVarTrans != "Identity") {
    dt1 <- AutoTransformationCreate(data = dt1, ColumnNames = XVar, Methods = XVarTrans)$Data
  }

  if(length(GroupVar) == 0L) {
    if(Debug) print('Scatter  length(GroupVar) == 0L')
    if(Debug) print('Scatter  Echarts')
    p1 <- echarts4r::e_charts_(
      dt1,
      x = XVar,
      dispose = TRUE,
      darkMode = TRUE,
      width = Width,
      height = Height)

    if(ShowLabels) {
      p1 <- echarts4r::e_scatter_3d_(e = p1, YVar, ZVar, ZVar, label = list(show = TRUE))

      p1 <- echarts4r::e_scatter_(e = p1, YVar)

        smooth = TRUE,

}

# Task: Implement AutoPlots Composite Prototype 1 — ImportancePareto()

Implement the first AutoPlots composite analytical view.

Start with ImportancePareto() only.

Do not implement the full composite framework yet.

Use the architecture recommended in docs/autoplots_composite_view_audit.md:
- named public composite function
- shared internal helper where useful
- reuse existing e_*_full() helpers/theme defaults where possible
- use raw echarts4r only where necessary
- avoid adding overlay flags to existing plot APIs

Objective:
Create a composite importance plot that shows:
- ranked importance bars
- cumulative contribution line
- optional cutoff/reference line
- clear labels/tooltips
- existing AutoPlots theme support
- human-readable default
- future-ready for LLM information encoding

Suggested API sketch:
ImportancePareto(
  data,
  XVar,
  YVar,
  TopN = 25,
  Sort = TRUE,
  Cumulative = TRUE,
  CumulativeAxis = "right",
  Cutoff = 0.8,
  Theme = "dark",
  Title = NULL,
  Subtitle = NULL,
  ...
)

Requirements:
- Accept common variable-importance style tables.
- Work with fake CatBoost-style importance tables.
- Preserve simple API defaults.
- Do not break existing AutoPlots functions.
- Add roxygen docs.
- Add examples.
- Add QA/smoke test.
- Add a small demo artifact if appropriate.
- Update composite audit doc with implementation notes.

Validation:
- source/package load passes.
- ImportancePareto() renders.
- Existing AutoPlots QA passes.
- No changes to existing APIs unless absolutely necessary.
- git diff --check passes.

Do not implement HistogramDensity(), ScatterSmooth(), BoxPlotSummary(), or SHAPDependenceBinned() yet.

\item{CumulativeAxis}{Side for the cumulative percentage axis. Use `"right"`

\item{Cutoff}{Optional cumulative reference line. Values between 0 and 1 are

\item{ShowLabels}{If TRUE, show labels on the importance bars.}

\item{MouseScroll}{If TRUE, use inside data zoom. Otherwise, show external
data zoom controls.}

\item{Encoding}{Reserved for future consumer-aware information encoding.
Current supported values are `"human"`, `"llm"`, `"thumbnail"`,
`"presentation"`, `"executive"`, and `"developer"`.}

\item{Debug}{If TRUE, print intermediate diagnostics.}

\item{...}{Reserved for future extensions.}
}

An echarts4r widget.
}

bars with a cumulative contribution line. This is useful for understanding
both the top drivers and how quickly their combined contribution approaches
the total importance.
}

# Task: Formalize the Marginal Information Gain (MIG) Framework

## Background

Over the course of developing Analytics Workstation, a more fundamental optimization objective has emerged.

Originally the goal appeared to be:

"Reduce token usage."

That is no longer the objective.

The objective is:

Maximize analytical information transfer while minimizing unnecessary cost.

This is conceptually identical to many optimization problems already familiar to data science:

- marginal ROI
- marginal lift
- marginal utility
- marginal media contribution
- efficient frontier
- diminishing returns

The same philosophy now applies to analytical evidence.

Every artifact should be viewed as an investment.

The question is not:

"Should this artifact be included?"

The question is:

"What is the marginal analytical information gained by including this artifact, given everything already selected?"

This becomes the governing optimization principle for Context Optimization.

---

# Objective

Create a new architectural document:

docs/marginal_information_gain_framework.md

This document should become one of the foundational architectural references of Analytics Workstation.

Do not implement production behavior.

Do not modify routing.

Formalize the theory.

---

# Core Principle

Every artifact contributes:

positive information

negative information

redundancy

cost

uncertainty

The optimization problem becomes:

Maximize:

Marginal Information Gain

Subject to:

token budget

latency budget

privacy

provider capability

user preference

decision criticality

---

# Define Marginal Information Gain

Describe MIG conceptually.

Examples:

An artifact contributes information only if it changes expected understanding.

If two artifacts communicate essentially the same analytical fact, the second artifact has low marginal gain.

If an artifact reveals entirely new analytical behavior, it has high marginal gain.

Marginal gain depends on:

- the question
- artifacts already selected
- model capabilities
- user objectives

It is contextual.

---

# Proposed Utility Components

Discuss components such as:

task relevance

trustworthiness

novelty

expected insight gain

expected decision impact

context cost

uncertainty

redundancy

provider capability

Do not finalize equations.

Document them as research concepts.

---

# Information Compression

Formalize the hierarchy:

Raw Data

â†“

Statistical Summary

â ... [truncated]


## 593. 2026-07-09T06:44:32.758Z - current_projectless_megathread - tool_output

# Context Optimization Policy

Analytics Workstation treats context as an architectural resource.

Tokens, latency, attention, reasoning, and privacy all have cost. The objective is not to minimize tokens. The objective is to maximize analytical information transfer while minimizing unnecessary cost.

## Core Principle

Never spend probabilistic intelligence on deterministic knowledge.

Deterministic reasoning should always execute first. Only uncertainty should consume probabilistic reasoning.

## Governing Hierarchy

Every future GenAI feature should respect this order:

```text
Deterministic reasoning
-> Evidence Routing
-> Optional Local GenAI
-> Optional Paid GenAI
-> Final Reasoning
-> Observability
-> Future Learning
```

The system should become more efficient over time, not more magical.

## Layer 1: Deterministic Knowledge

The workstation should compute known facts before any LLM is involved.

Examples:

- constant variables
- near-zero variance
- missingness
- sparse groups
- correlation
- artifact quality
- screenshot availability
- render target
- producer metadata
- collector metadata
- routing profile
- provider capabilities
- context size estimation
- token estimation
- image capability
- safety limits

These checks should never require GenAI.

## Layer 2: Evidence Routing

Evidence Routing uses deterministic information to estimate:

- task relevance
- trustworthiness
- novelty
- expected insight gain
- expected context cost

It then assigns routing levels and builds Evidence Plans.

This remains deterministic and explainable. The evidence plan should answer:

- why was this artifact included?
- why was this artifact excluded?
- why was this artifact downgraded?
- why was this context strategy chosen?
- what evidence is missing?

## Layer 3: Probabilistic Routing

Probabilistic routing is optional.

It may be used only when deterministic routing cannot confidently choose between evidence candidates.

Appropriate uses:
. : File C:\Users\Bizon\Documents\WindowsPowerShell\profile.ps1 cannot be loaded. The file

## 594. 2026-07-09T06:44:32.758Z - current_projectless_megathread - tool_output

Output:
# Evidence Routing Policy

Analytics Workstation should not blindly send every artifact to a language model. It should build an evidence plan that explains what evidence is included, excluded, summarized, deep-dived, or kept as sidecar reference.

This first policy is conservative, rule-based, explainable, configurable, telemetry-rich, and learning-ready. It is not autonomous and does not mutate production behavior.

Evidence Routing is layer 2 of the broader Context Optimization Policy. Deterministic artifact facts, provider capabilities, quality metadata, and safety limits should be evaluated first. Evidence Routing then uses those deterministic inputs to build an explainable evidence plan before any optional probabilistic routing or final GenAI reasoning occurs.

## Evidence Plan

An evidence plan records:

- question
- task type
- routing profile
- provider and model
- user constraints
- selected artifacts
- excluded artifacts
- mention-only or sidecar-only artifacts
- deep-dive artifacts
- request-more-evidence rows
- context strategy per artifact
- routing reason
- expected utility
- estimated context cost
- confidence
- fallback strategy

## Utility Model

The first-pass utility score is:

```text
artifact_utility =
task_relevance
* trustworthiness
* novelty
* expected_insight_gain
* user_preference_weight
/ estimated_context_cost
```

The score is intentionally approximate. It exists to produce inspectable routing decisions, not to pretend the system has learned optimal behavior.

## Routing Levels

0. Exclude
1. Mention Only
2. Summary
3. Evidence
4. Deep Dive
5. Request More Evidence

Every candidate receives one level and a reason.

## Profiles

Supported routing profiles:

- `conservative`
- `balanced`
- `thorough`
- `accuracy_first`
- `token_saver`
- `vision_first`
- `local_private`

Profiles configure artifact count, image/table limits, token budget, deep-dive threshold, redundancy tolerance, and preference for vision or exact values.

## Evidence Strategy Layer

User-facing Evidence Strategies map business intent to routing configuration:

- Efficient
- Balanced
- Thorough
- Critical Decision
- Cost Is Irrelevant

## 595. 2026-07-09T06:46:13.828Z - current_projectless_megathread - tool_output

Exit code: 0

Output:
# Marginal Information Gain Framework

Analytics Workstation treats analytical evidence as an optimization problem.

The original intuition was simple: reduce token usage. That is no longer the right objective. A token-minimizing system can become under-informed, brittle, and misleading. A maximum-context system can become expensive, slow, redundant, and harder for a language model to reason over.

The deeper objective is:

```text
Maximize analytical information transfer
while minimizing unnecessary cost.
```

This is the same pattern that appears throughout data science and decision science:

- marginal ROI
- marginal lift
- marginal utility
- marginal media contribution
- efficient frontiers
- diminishing returns
- budget-constrained optimization

Analytics Workstation applies the same reasoning to analytical evidence.

Every artifact is an investment. The question is not simply:

```text
Should this artifact be included?
```

The better question is:

```text
What is the marginal analytical information gained by including this artifact,
given the evidence already selected and the decision being supported?
```

That question defines the Marginal Information Gain framework.

## Core Principle

Every artifact can contribute:

- positive information
- negative information
- redundancy
- cost
- uncertainty

Positive information helps the consumer understand the analytical landscape more accurately. Negative information can also be valuable: a caveat, warning, failed validation, model weakness, missingness pattern, suspicious leakage signal, or fragile assumption may reduce confidence in a conclusion, but it improves the truthfulness of the evidence package.

Redundancy is not automatically bad. Repeated evidence can increase confidence when it comes from independent perspectives. But redundancy becomes wasteful when it repeats the same analytical fact without improving trust, nuance, or decision quality.

Cost is broader than tokens. Cost includes:

- input tokens
- output tokens
- latency
- provider cost
. : File C:\Users\Bizon\Documents\WindowsPowerShell\profile.ps1 cannot be loaded. The file 
C:\Users\Bizon\Documents\WindowsPowerShell\profile.ps1 is not digitally signed. You cannot run this script on the 
current system. For more information about running scripts and setting execution policy, see about_Execution_Policies 
at https:/go.microsoft.com/fwlink/?LinkID=135170.
